title: Implement --sdnotify cmdline option to control sd-notify behavior
body:
--sdnotify conmon-only|container|none

With "conmon-only", we send the MAINPID, and clear the NOTIFY_SOCKET so the OCI
runtime doesn't pass it into the container. We also advertise "ready" when the
OCI runtime finishes to advertise the service as ready.

With "container", we send the MAINPID, and leave the NOTIFY_SOCKET so the OCI
runtime passes it into the container for initialization, and let the container advertise further metadata.

The "none" option does what it's always done in the past - passes NOTIFY_SOCKET
to conmon's process and the OCI runtime processes, and does no manipulation or "help".

This removes the need for hardcoded CID and PID files in the command line, and
the PIDFile directive, as the pid is advertised directly through sd-notify.

Signed-off-by: Joseph Gooch <mrwizard@dok.org>

Includes #6689 
References #6688

comments.body:
Hi @goochjj. Thanks for your PR.

I'm waiting for a [containers](https://github.com/orgs/containers/people) member to verify that this patch is reasonable to test. If it is, they should reply with `/ok-to-test` on its own line. Until that is done, I will not automatically test new commits in this PR, but the usual testing commands by org members will still work. Regular contributors should [join the org](https://github.com/orgs/containers/people) to skip this step.

Once the patch is verified, the new status will be reflected by the `ok-to-test` label.

I understand the commands that are listed [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod).

<details>

Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes/test-infra](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>

The more I think about this I'm not sure "none" is a valid option, though it was essentially requested...  Since "none" returns a broken situation. (MAINPID never advertised properly)... Unless there's a case when runc or crun advertises the correct MAINPID.

I think `none` is "I am not being managed by systemd, please ignore" - we've seen cases where Podman is run as a child of something actually managed by systemd, inherits NOTIFY_SOCKET from the parent (arguably a bug in the parent, but still), and then blocks forever as it tries to notify on an already-used socket.

> I think `none` is "I am not being managed by systemd, please ignore" - we've seen cases where Podman is run as a child of something actually managed by systemd, inherits NOTIFY_SOCKET from the parent (arguably a bug in the parent, but still), and then blocks forever as it tries to notify on an already-used socket.

That's a reasonable interpretation.  In that case I should probably block NOTIFY_SOCKET from being passed down to the OCI runtime and conmon... correct?  That way everything will proceed as if the NOTIFY_SOCKET isn't there.

vs. what it does now, which means "libpod does nothing with NOTIFY_SOCKET other than pass it on", which is pretty similar to "container".

> > I think `none` is "I am not being managed by systemd, please ignore" - we've seen cases where Podman is run as a child of something actually managed by systemd, inherits NOTIFY_SOCKET from the parent (arguably a bug in the parent, but still), and then blocks forever as it tries to notify on an already-used socket.
> 
> That's a reasonable interpretation. In that case I should probably block NOTIFY_SOCKET from being passed down to the OCI runtime and conmon... correct? That way everything will proceed as if the NOTIFY_SOCKET isn't there.
> 
> vs. what it does now, which means "libpod does nothing with NOTIFY_SOCKET other than pass it on", which is pretty similar to "container".

In which case I think "ignore" is a better description.
Ignore works for me - and yes, we should not forward the socket in that case

Doesn't Podman have to respond to the ignore and set the ready state for systemd if it sees the flag and is set to ignore?  IE Systemd will never mark the unit file as ready unless someone says it is ready.

> Doesn't Podman have to respond to the ignore and set the ready state for systemd if it sees the flag and is set to ignore? IE Systemd will never mark the unit file as ready unless someone says it is ready.

That's true - however, currently Podman doesn't respond at all.  Runc/crun do.  Ignore is only relevant when some other service is the responsible party.


The default is "container", which is the current behavior, which would mean Runc/Crun need to respond.  This PR just makes sure the correct MAINPID is set during the conversation.

conmon-only means Podman is the responsible party - the one responsible for sending READY=1, and keeps runc/crun out of the loop.

ignore would only be used if some other service is calling podman. I.e. some service that is sd-notify enabled, and therefore they are the responsible party.  Contrived example:
```
#!/bin/bash

#I'm a service
systemd-notify --status="Starting up"

#Spawn some containers
podman run --sdnotify ignore --rm -d alpine sleep infinity
podman run --sdnotify ignore --rm -d alpine sleep infinity
podman run --sdnotify ignore --rm -d alpine sleep infinity

#Do other stuff
sleep 1

#And we're good
systemd-notify --ready
```

Right now, each podman would see the NOTIFY_SOCKET set and assume that it's responsible for telling systemd when it's ready... Which is incorrect, the overall service is the shell script. 

The other alternative is people explicitly changing the podman lines to do:
```
env -u NOTIFY_SOCKET podman run --rm -d alpine sleep infinity
```

As that's really what ignore is doing.

> This removes the need for hardcoded CID and PID files in the command line, and
the PIDFile directive, as the pid is advertised directly through sd-notify.

Can you elaborate why this would remove the CID file? I think that's still needed to cleanly stop/remove a container in a unit.
Reviewing now. @giuseppe PTAL as well
Once comments are addressed, this LGTM

> > This removes the need for hardcoded CID and PID files in the command line, and
> > the PIDFile directive, as the pid is advertised directly through sd-notify.
> 
> Can you elaborate why this would remove the CID file? I think that's still needed to cleanly stop/remove a container in a unit.

CID file can still be used, it's just SystemD doesn't need it so it doesn't need to be hanging out in /run/%n-cid, it'll revert to being in userdata/

PID file can still be used, it's just SystemD doesn't need it so it doesn't need to be hanging out in /run/%n.pid, it'll revert to being in userdata/.... And we don't need to specify it in PIDFile because systemd will receive the appropriate PID via MAINPID= w/ Type=notify.

If for whatever reason people still want to use Type=forking, they can, they'll need to keep the PIDFile directive and put it somewhere it can be found... Otherwise Type=notify just makes things more clean and less hardcoded.

> Oh man, I really like this PR. Thanks a ton!
> 
> Just a minor comment regarding input validation. @edsantiago , could you have a look as well? I wonder if you have a suggestion how we could test this in CI.

I too was struggling with this.  I can envision the tests - as I did the testing manually, but it's going to require forking which is currently beyond my ability to do in golang. 

We'd need a thread that opens a UNIX dgram socket and captures input.  Perhaps (tmpdir)/notify.

1) Ensure podman ignores the socket
  Create notify socket
  Monitor notify socket
  set env NOTIFY_SOCKET
  Spawn podman run --rm --sd-notify=ignore alpine ls
  Verify podman returns exit 0
  Verify notify socket received absolutely no data.

2) Verify sd-notify=conmon (heres where it starts to get tricky)
  Create notify socket
  set env NOTIFY_SOCKET
  Proc1) Monitor notify socket
  Proc2) Spawn podman run --rm --sd-notify=conmon alpine ls
  Proc1) wait for receipt of MAINPID=(pid) and verify it points to conmon, descendent of Proc2
  Proc1) Wait for receipt of second MAINPID= and READY=1
  Proc2) verify exit 0

3) Verify sd-notify=container
  Create notify socket
  set env NOTIFY_SOCKET
  Proc1) Monitor notify socket
  Proc2) Spawn podman run --rm --sd-notify=container alpine ls
  Proc1) wait for receipt of MAINPID=(pid) and verify it points to conmon, descendent of Proc2
  Proc1) sleep 3 seconds
  Proc1) Find the socket - /run/runc/(CID)/notify/notify.sock or /run/crun/(CID)/notify/notify - and send READY=1 to it
  Proc1) Verify you get back MAINPID= and READY=1
  Proc2) verify exit 0

  If podman exits in Proc2 before we send the READY in Proc1, then runc/crun didn't wait for a notification.

4) Test that not specifying sd-notify does the same thing as 3
5) Test that not setting NOTIFY_SOCKET does the same thing as 1

I'm not sure how to coordinate the test between two threads like that in Go.
All comments should be resolved at this point. PTAL!

I'm having a lot of trouble with `sdnotify=container`. It is very possible that the fault is mine, because it's been many months since I last fiddled with sdnotify.

I think someone/something (crun?) is intercepting `NOTIFY_SOCKET`. Note these two commands, differing only in the setting of that envariable:
```console
$ env -u NOTIFY_SOCKET ./bin/podman run --privileged --rm -v /tmp/podman-sdnotify:/tmp/podman-sdnotify:Z alpine ls -l /tmp
total 0
srwxrwxr-x    1 root     root             0 Jun 22 17:24 podman-sdnotify
$ NOTIFY_SOCKET=/tmp/podman-sdnotify ./bin/podman run --privileged --rm -v /tmp/podman-sdnotify:/tmp/podman-sdnotify:Z alpine ls -l /tmp
total 0
srwxrwxrwx    1 root     root             0 Jun 22 17:36 notify
```
That is: when `NOTIFY_SOCKET` is unset, the (existing) socket is bind-mounted just fine in the container. When it is **set**, the bind mount doesn't work, and there's a socket called `notify`.

The context for this is that I was trying:
```console
$ socat unix-recv:/tmp/podman-sdnotify -

[in another terminal]
$ ./bin/podman run --privileged --rm --sdnotify=container -v /tmp/podman-sdnotify:/tmp/podman-sdnotify:Z fedora systemd-notify --ready
Failed to notify init system: No such file or directory
```
I have a vague memory that this was intentional, but don't remember the details. @giuseppe perhaps you remember?

> I'm having a lot of trouble with `sdnotify=container`. It is very possible that the fault is mine, because it's been many months since I last fiddled with sdnotify.

> $ NOTIFY_SOCKET=/tmp/podman-sdnotify ./bin/podman run --privileged --rm -v /tmp/podman-sdnotify:/tmp/podman-sdnotify:Z alpine ls -l /tmp
> total 0
> srwxrwxrwx    1 root     root             0 Jun 22 17:36 notify
> ```

> Failed to notify init system: No such file or directory
> ```
> 
> I have a vague memory that this was intentional, but don't remember the details. @giuseppe perhaps you remember?

runc/crun take care of proxying the socket. In fact they don't bind mount it, they create a unix-datagram inside the container, and write to the one outside.

I am attempting to decipher goroutines so I can write a proper test in e2e...

Basically though:
T1: `socat unix-recvfrom:/tmp/podman-sdnotify,fork -`
T2: `env NOTIFY_SOCKET=/tmp/podman-sdnotify ./bin/podman run --rm --sdnotify=container fedora systemd-notify --ready`

T1 shows
MAINPID=22842READY=1
MAINPID=22854
MAINPID=22842

This illustrates the sequence:
  podman now sends 22842 (conmon) PID during oci_conmon_linux
  runc/crun passes READY=1 back from systemd-notify
  runc/crun passes MAINPID=22854 back from systemd-notify (which we can't monitor)
  podman sends 22842 from container start - to overwrite the wrong PID

```
 MAINPID=22842READY=1
```
How are you getting this?! My entire rathole stemmed from my trying to do exactly that. I cannot get it to work, either root or rootless: `systemd-notify` in the container fails as I described above. I get `MAINPID=xxx` in the `socat` window, but never `READY=1`
I'm on Fedora 32 with crun

correction - 

When NOTIFY_SOCKET is passed,
1) runc will create a socket in /run/runc/CID/notify/notify.sock, and then add a bind mount to /run/systemd/notify.sock (or similar).  This works as I illustrated
2) crun is creating a socket in /run/crun/CID/notify/notify.sock, but never adds the bind mount, so it crashes spectacularly.

Note that's not a problem with this PR, it's crun missing a bind mount - try on podman pre-this-PR without a sdnotify command line option - that fails too.

Just using your technique of adding the bind mount won't work - because runc and crun are explicitly blocking until THEIR socket receives a READY=1.

Heh, this is weird.  It's apparently assuming the socket name is "notify".

When I pass /run/podman-sdnotify, it's mounted as /run/notify
When I pass /tmp/podman-sdnotify, it's mounted as /tmp/notify

I'm assuming this has just been working, because systemd's socket filename is notify. sooooo

> >

> When I pass /tmp/podman-sdnotify, it's mounted as /tmp/notify
> 
> I'm assuming this has just been working, because systemd's socket filename is notify. sooooo

both crun and runc wrap the NOTIFY_SOCKET logic and expose a different socket to the container.

When the container notifies it is ready, crun/runc reads the notification on the socket they've created and propagate it back to systemd on the correct socket.

This is to address two issues:

- workaround a systemd notify socket race where the sender of the notification cannot be correctly found with short lived processes if it is not directly monitored by systemd or in a different cgroup.

- avoid that the container communicates directly with systemd and could send some weird MAINPID= value.  So both crun/runc filter anything received from the container except `READY=1`.  I think systemd-nspawn does the same thing.

> > > 
> > 
> > 
> > Heh, this is weird. It's apparently assuming the socket name is "notify".
> > When I pass /run/podman-sdnotify, it's mounted as /run/notify
> > When I pass /tmp/podman-sdnotify, it's mounted as /tmp/notify
> > I'm assuming this has just been working, because systemd's socket filename is notify. sooooo
> 
> both crun and runc wrap the NOTIFY_SOCKET logic and expose a different socket to the container.
> 
> When the container notifies it is ready, crun/runc reads the notification on the socket they've created and propagate it back to systemd on the correct socket.
> 
> This is to address two issues:
> 
> * workaround a systemd notify socket race where the sender of the notification cannot be correctly found with short lived processes if it is not directly monitored by systemd or in a different cgroup.
> * avoid that the container communicates directly with systemd and could send some weird MAINPID= value.  So both crun/runc filter anything received from the container except `READY=1`.  I think systemd-nspawn does the same thing.

I have no problem with any of that, except crun is doing it wrong.

When NOTIFY_SOCKET=/run/podman-sdnotify is passed,
CRUN creates /run/crun/CID/notify/notify.sock
CRUN bind mounts /run/crun/CID/notify/notify.sock to /run/notify
CRUN starts the container with NOTIFY_SOCKET=/run/podman-sdnotify

And it doesn't work.

Bind mount appears to be mounting to $(dirname $NOTIFY_SOCKET)/notify but passes the NOTIFY_SOCKET as is.

So, runc works regardless of what NOTIFY_SOCKET is
crun only works if the socket name is notify.  i.e. /run/systemd/notify, /run/notify, /tmp/notify, /tmp/some/path/here/notify, what have you.

Since this isn't related to my PR, I don't think it should be a blocker on this issue - if it needs to be corrected there'd need to be an issue in the crun repo.

@giuseppe, could you take a look at what crun is doing here, and how it's mounting the socket? In particular:
```console
$ NOTIFY_SOCKET=/tmp/podman-sdnotify/notify ./bin/podman run --rm --sdnotify=container fedora sh -c 'ls -lZ /tmp'
total 4
-rwx------. 1 root root system_u:object_r:fusefs_t:s0       1379 May 15 06:48 ks-script-xz5dtd94
drwx------. 2 root root unconfined_u:object_r:user_tmp_t:s0   60 Jun 25 12:17 podman-sdnotify

$ NOTIFY_SOCKET=/tmp/podman-sdnotify/notify ./bin/podman run --rm --sdnotify=container fedora sh -c 'ls -lZ /tmp/podman-sdnotify'
ls: cannot open directory '/tmp/podman-sdnotify': Permission denied

$ sudo setenforce 0
$ NOTIFY_SOCKET=/tmp/podman-sdnotify/notify ./bin/podman run --rm --sdnotify=container fedora sh -c 'ls -lZ /tmp/podman-sdnotify'
total 0
srwxrwxrwx. 1 root root unconfined_u:object_r:user_tmp_t:s0 0 Jun 25 12:17 notify
```
This happens if I try `/run/user/MYID/systemd/notify` also: the parent directory and socket are created with `user_tmp_t`, and I get an AVC when the container tries to access the socket.

> @giuseppe, could you take a look at what crun is doing here, and how it's mounting the socket? In particular:

crun is mounting the socket from the `/run/user/$UID` directory.  That is a socket created by crun itself.  crun will read from this socket and propagate the notification to systemd.

That is why we see the `unconfined_u:object_r:container_var_run_t:s0` label.  Should we relax the selinux policy?  I'll try to change the label from crun, I'll let you know in a bit

I've opened a PR here: https://github.com/containers/crun/pull/419

total 0

@edsantiago This is how I'm currently testing

[notifytesting.zip](https://github.com/containers/libpod/files/4858525/notifytesting.zip)

For container, I'll test rootless with
bash notifytest-container.sh $HOME/tmp/notify.sock
bash notifytest-container.sh $HOME/tmp/a/long/path/notify.sock

Root
bash notifytest-container.sh /run/notify.sock
bash notifytest-container.sh /run/notify/notify.sock
bash notifytest-container.sh /tmp/no/particular/path/and/name

The others can run root or rootless since no bind mount takes place.

The --pids-limit 0 is an artifact of #6834

@edsantiago place your podman in /usr/local/bin, chcon it to match /usr/bin/
Actually, `sudo make install.bin-nobuild`
And then use /usr/local/bin.

The SELinux policy tags the podman process based on its source location

Argh - missing `chcon`, that was it. Thank you!

PS location doesn't matter, all you need to do is `chcon -t container_runtime_exec_t bin/podman`
Interesting, when I didn't run it from /usr/local I don't think I got the process context, just the file context.  <shrug> I'm only pretending to know what I'm doing.

Should we maybe check crun and container-selinux directly? i.e
```
. /etc/os-release
if [ "$NAME" = "Fedora" -a "$VERSION_ID" = "32" ]; then
  VER=$(rpm -q container-selinux --queryformat "%{VERSION}")
  if [ -z "$VER" ]; then echo "Cannot find container-selinux"; exit 1; fi
  rpmdev-vercmp 2.137 "$VER"
  RET=$?
  if [ "$RET" = "12" ]; then
    #Package is 2.137.0 or newer
    true
  else
    echo "Container-selinux is older than 2.137, skipping test"
  fi
fi
```
